perturbation.original_text: 4. Compensation. In consideration of the services to be rendered by Maimon hereunder and for Maimon's service as a director on the Board of Directors of the Company, the Company will pay Maimon a monthly fee of $1,000 during the Minimum Period, beginning on the Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Compensation. In consideration of the services to be rendered by Maimon hereunder and for Maimon's service as a director on the Board of Directors of the Company, the Company *may* pay Maimon a monthly *stipend* of $1,000 during the Minimum Period, beginning on the Effective Date.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will pay' to 'may pay' and 'monthly fee' to 'monthly stipend' introduces ambiguity regarding whether Maimon is guaranteed payment for his services or whether the company has discretion over payment. In addition, the use of 'stipend' instead of 'fee' implies that the payment is not necessarily tied to the value of the services rendered, leading to uncertainty in enforcement. This contradicts the initial statement in the agreement stating that it is in consideration for services to be rendered, implying a guarantee of payment.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. Compensation. In consideration of the services to be rendered by Maimon hereunder and for Maimon's service as a director on the Board of Directors of the Company, the Company *may* pay Maimon a monthly *stipend* of $1,000 during the Minimum Period, beginning on the Effective Date.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that the company 'will pay' Maimon, which creates a contractual obligation. The changed text modifies this to 'may pay', introducing discretion where there was previously a requirement. This contradicts the initial premise that the payment is 'in consideration of the services to be rendered,' because if the company 'may' pay, it is not necessarily tied to the services, and thus, there's no guarantee Maimon will be compensated, leading to ambiguity and a potential in-text contradiction. In addition, changing the payment from 'monthly fee' to 'monthly stipend' creates further ambiguity regarding whether the payment is tied to the value of services rendered.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Termination. If Maimon should become unable to serve as CEO, or should fail to perform any of the obligations hereunder for any cause including death or disability, always in the sole judgment and decision of the Company, then the Company shall have the right to terminate this agreement on five days prior written notice. Maimon shall have the right to resign at any time upon 30 days prior written notice. The Company may in its discretion and at its option terminate this Agreement at any time after the Minimum Period upon five days prior written notice to Maimon.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Termination. If Maimon should become unable to serve as CEO, or should fail to perform any of the obligations hereunder for any cause including death or disability, always in the sole judgment and decision of the Company, then the Company *reserves the option* to terminate this agreement on five days prior written notice. Maimon *retains the option* to resign at any time upon 30 days prior written notice. The Company *is required to* terminate this Agreement at any time after the Minimum Period upon five days prior written notice to Maimon.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall have the right' to 'reserves the option', 'shall have the right' to 'retains the option' and 'may in its discretion and at its option terminate' to 'is required to terminate' introduces ambiguity and contradiction regarding termination rights of both parties. Firstly the change implies that the company doesn't hold a 'right', rather it 'reserves an option'. This also applies to the employees and the action is changed from right to option. On the other hand, changing the termination after the minimum period to 'is required to' when previously it stated 'may at its discretion and option' creates a contradiction on whether termination after the minimum period is required.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Termination. If Maimon should become unable to serve as CEO, or should fail to perform any of the obligations hereunder for any cause including death or disability, always in the sole judgment and decision of the Company, then the Company *reserves the option* to terminate this agreement on five days prior written notice. Maimon *retains the option* to resign at any time upon 30 days prior written notice. The Company *is required to* terminate this Agreement at any time after the Minimum Period upon five days prior written notice to Maimon.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction. The original text specifies 'shall have the right' for Maimon's resignation and company's termination, implying a definite right. The modified text changes this to 'retains the option' and 'reserves the option' respectively, weakening the definitive right into an option, introducing ambiguity regarding the strength of the right to terminate/resign. Moreover, the original text states that the company 'may in its discretion and at its option terminate', whereas the new text states that the company 'is required to terminate', creating a direct conflict between the two versions.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Confidential Information. Maimon recognizes and acknowledges that by reason of his retention by and service to the Company, Maimon will have access to certain confidential and proprietary information relating to the Company's business, which may include, but is not limited to, trade secrets, trade "know-how," product development techniques and plans, formulas, customer lists and addresses, financing services, funding programs, cost and pricing information, marketing and sales techniques, strategy and programs, computer programs and software and financial information (collectively referred to as "Confidential Information").
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Confidential Information. Maimon recognizes and acknowledges that by reason of his retention by and service to the Company, Maimon will have access to certain *company* information relating to the Company's business, which may include, but is not limited to, trade secrets, trade "know-how," product development techniques and plans, formulas, customer lists and addresses, financing services, funding programs, cost and pricing information, marketing and sales techniques, strategy and programs, computer programs and software and financial information (collectively referred to as "Confidential Information").
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'confidential and proprietary information' to 'company information' but still referring to it as 'Confidential Information' within the parentheses introduces a contradiction. The text implies that all company information is considered confidential, which is untrue and opens it for uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. Confidential Information. Maimon recognizes and acknowledges that by reason of his retention by and service to the Company, Maimon will have access to certain *company* information relating to the Company's business, which may include, but is not limited to, trade secrets, trade "know-how," product development techniques and plans, formulas, customer lists and addresses, financing services, funding programs, cost and pricing information, marketing and sales techniques, strategy and programs, computer programs and software and financial information (collectively referred to as "Confidential Information").
perturbation.justification: The change from 'confidential and proprietary information' to 'company information' creates a strong contradiction because it redefines the scope of what is considered 'Confidential Information' without updating the term's application. The contradiction arises because the modified text implies that all 'company information,' including potentially mundane details, is treated as 'Confidential Information,' conflicting with the reasonable expectation that only genuinely sensitive data would be subject to confidentiality obligations as defined later in the contract. This broadens the definition in a way that makes enforcement unclear, as it's unreasonable and likely unenforceable to treat every piece of company information as confidential. This directly clashes with the purpose and enforceability of the 'Confidential Information' clause, creating ambiguity about what information the recipient is actually bound to protect. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition, as it uses 'company information' interchangeably with 'Confidential Information' but without a proper definition, leading to conflicting obligations.
